course_description:
This course provides an introduction to the underlying principles of service management in the tourism, hospitality and event industries. It is focused on the strategies, systems, processes and design aspects of creating and evaluating service delivery, service excellence and service orientation. Topics include understanding the `service-dominant logic' approach to marketing and managing service, setting service strategy, understanding customer needs, building a service-minded workforce, measuring and evaluating service quality, and drivers of service industry customer loyalty.

Welcome to TOUR7021. This course could have any of a number of different course titles, any of which could give further clues about the basis for it. Other titles could be, "Management: A Service Perspective", or "Management Principles for Service Industries", or "Principles for Managing Customer Service", or simply "Service Excellence: A Managerial Approach".

As a postgraduate student, you will appreciate this course, as it will help you address the various management tasks in the hospitality/tourism/events sector from a service perspective.

This is a core course in the Master of Tourism, Hotelﾠand Event Management - and plays a central role in providing students a sound managerial underpinning to the tourism and related sectors. It connects traditional management theory and practice with important distinctions relevant to organisations where service is vital to the offering and to customer value creation. Upon successful completion of this course, students will have a broadened sense of managerial responsibilities required in managing and leading service organisations.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

An industry guest speaker has been added (subject to availability) to provide students with first-hand THE industry updates and information.

Also a scaffolded approach to developing students' critical thinking, presentation skills and academic writing as well as team work skills continues to be revised and improved, particularly in line with emerging technologies.

Sustainable Development Goals- UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of theUnited Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME). As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through theSustainable Development Goals. The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

course_aims: This course provides an introduction to the underlying principles of service management in the tourism, hospitality and event industries, aiming to provide insights into the strategies, systems, processes and design aspects of creatingand evaluating service delivery, service excellence and service orientation. After completing this course students will have a broader and deeper understanding of management, marketing and human resource implications for managing serviceorganisations and for the effective delivery of high quality customer service specific to various segments of the market.

learning_outcomes.description: Evaluate tourism, hospitality and event management from a service management perspective.
Explore the critical role of managing the employee-customer interface in the delivery of service.
Recognise, evaluate and recommend key service management systems and processes to improve or solve problems in complex service environments.
Understand current best practice in relation to measuring business performance for service-related businesses.
Demonstrate the capacity to critically evaluate self and others.

assessment_details.title: Service Experience Oral Pitch
assessment_details.task_description: There are many concepts that underpin service management. This task requires you to reflect upon your own service experiences on a daily basis, from a customer perspective, and then relate your experience to a relevant service management concept.This will be a 3-minute individual presentation delivered live during our class. It should provide sufficient description of a service experience that you have recently experienced. One photo taken by you is required to help explain the service experience to the rest of the students. This will be shared on the screen during your pitch.Your pitch should cover three vital aspects:Date, time and place of the service experience.A brief description of the service experience (so that the audience can understand enough to know if the link to the service concept is appropriate).A justification of the relevance between the experience and the assigned service management concept. These concepts will be allocated in class in Week 2. (i.e. every student will be given a concept on which to base their presentation).The Pitch should be clear and coherent; propose a clear position of your experience as a customer stakeholder and be engaging. There is no written component, but you must submit your Photo to Turnitin.Please Note:The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.AI StatementArtificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via Turnitin
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extensions or deferrals are not available for an in-class presentation. An extension may be available for the submitted material only.
Late submission
Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero.

assessment_details.title: Service Experience Report
assessment_details.task_description: There are many concepts that underpin service management. This assignment requires you to reflect upon your own service experiences on a daily basis, from a customer perspective, and then critically analyse one of these experiences through the lens of a relevant service management concept.Students will share their individual service experience (from Assignment 1) through a 2000 word Report, including an Executive Summary, Service Experience Overview, Literature Review and Recommendations.In your Service Experience Oral Pitch (Assessment 1) you related one of your own service experiences to a service management concept.In this assessment (Service Experience Report) you will critically analyse the same service experience through the lens of a relevant service management concept.The service management concepts that can be used will be discussed in class and include: Service Characteristics, Service Quality and Dual Value Creation.You should provide sufficient description of a service experience that you have recently experienced. However, the main emphasis should be on the clear connection between ‘what happened’ and a relevant service concept. Using the concept, you should critically analyse the experience, and depending on the context, offer insights, draw a conclusion and/or offer recommendations.The target audience for your Report is industry managers who should benefit from your insights.Important: The main emphasis is on your ability to apply service management concepts in a real life context.AI STATEMENTArtificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submitted via Turnitin
Deferral or extension
You may be able toapply for an extension.
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
Apenaltyof 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure.See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Task Description:This is a problem-based assessment whereby students will have to apply service management principles, theories and approaches to address real-world situations and challenges facing all service industries - with a focus on the tourism, hospitality and events sectors. The assessment will assess your understanding of the content from all modules we have covered this semester.This is a 2 hour exam paper with 10 minutes reading time. The format of the assessment includes short answer questions.More details will be provided in class, as well as preparation advice.Criteria & Marking:Further information will be provided on Blackboard and in class.AI STATEMENTThis assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description

learning_activities.topic: TOPIC 1: INTRODUCTION/BACKGROUNDThis first week will introduce students to the course including the teaching philosophy of the course coordinator and the teaching team. We will outline our expectations of students, provide an overview of assessment and clarify timelines. We will also summarise the course journey, how it fits into the wider program and business foundational courses.Learning outcomes:L01

TOPIC 2: FOUNDATIONS OF SERVICE MANAGEMENTThis week we will explore how management theory has developed and evolved from a product to a service to an experience economy. We will examine the Engle Curve and provide various foundation definitions and concepts including service dominant logic and dual value co-creation. More assessment Information will be provided especially in relation to the Service Experience Pitch oral presentation task.Learning outcomes:L01, L03

TOPIC 3: SATISFACTION, LOYALTY, QUALITYWe now focus on differences in quality perceptions in service/product management. We make connections to satisfaction and loyalty; different types of loyalty and links to service; how to measure service quality; differences between process and outcome dimensions. We will have a class activity based on a case Study of the Gronroos Article.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L03, L05

TOPIC 4: CUSTOMER ORIENTATION (PART 1)This week we introduce the very important concept of customer orientation by understanding the importance for service organisations to have a customer centred approach. We will look at different types of customers, examples of customer vs. product centred management and conduct a class activity based on the Case study of the Parasuraman et al Article.Learning outcomes:L01, L03

TOPIC 4: CUSTOMER ORIENTATION (PART 2)This week continues the examination of Customer Orientation Part 2. We will talk about customer research techniques such as listening systems, mystery shoppers, employee surveys and focus groups. Our class case study will be based on the Shah et al Article.Learning outcomes:L01, L03

TOPIC 5: SERVICE ENCOUNTER & CUSTOMER JOURNEY MAPFor this week, we break down the employee-customer-organisational interface, the service encounter 'triad' and its challenges and solutions. Bring your creativity because students will create their own customer journey maps. We will also provide some presentation tips for the first assessment.Learning outcomes:L01, L03

TOPIC 6: SUPPLY, DEMAND & PSYCHOLOGY OF WAITINGHere we turn our attention to the challenges of unmatched supply and demand, examine management strategies, and how to manage supply and demand variances. We also investigate the fascinating topic of Psychology of Waiting and managing real/perceived waits. We will also have our Pitch Presentations in class.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L03

TOPIC 7: SERVICE VALUES & VISIONIt is important to understand the differences in vision, mission, strategy and values for service organisations. This week we will discuss these differences with many examples. We will also look at challenges for service organisations in creating service oriented values. We will continue with the Pitch Presentations if needed.Learning outcomes:L01, L03, L04, L05

TOPIC 8: SERVICE PROFIT CHAINThis week reviews the historical and conceptual development of a very critical concept in service management known as the SPC. We will probe internal service quality, organisational psychology for service firms and employee engagement. There will also be a very important Report Writing Workshop to help with the next assignment.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L03, L04

TOPIC 9: SERVICE GUARANTEESService guarantees are vital for good customer service and this week we provide a justification for implementing guarantees in service contexts. We examine the nature and characteristics of effective guarantees through interactive classroom activities.Learning outcomes:L01, L03

TOPIC 10: INDUSTRY INSIGHTS OF SERVICE MANAGEMENT CHALLENGESThis week offers an opportunity to hear first hand some THE Industry insights into meeting common service management challenges. Guest Speaker - subject to availability.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L04

TOPIC 11: SERVICE FAILURE/RECOVERYThis will be our last week of course content and we define service failure and service recovery by examining how great service firms use this as competitive advantage. Also, we look at the interesting phenomenon of the service recovery paradox. We will also have a class discussion based on the famous Danny Meyer Case Study.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L03, L04
OVERVIEW AND SUMMARYOur last week includes a final review and consolidation of material as we help you prepare for your Final Assessment.Learning outcomes:L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
